questions.question: Rf value is less than one because

answers.a: Distance covered by the solute is more than solvent front
answers.b: Distance covered by solvent front is more than distance covered by solute

explanations.a: Incorrect. The solute cannot travel farther than the solvent front.
explanations.b: Correct. Rf is the ratio of distance traveled by the solute to the distance traveled by the solvent front, so it is usually less than 1.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Only the second statement is true.
explanations.d: Incorrect. One of the statements is correct.

questions.question: Rf value is close to 1, then what happened?

answers.c: Separation does not depend on Rf

explanations.a: Incorrect. Very high Rf values generally indicate poor separation.
explanations.b: Correct. When the spot is too close to the solvent front, separation is usually poor.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Rf values influence chromatographic separation.

explanations.a: This factor affects Rf values but is not the only one.
explanations.b: This factor affects Rf values but is not the only one.
explanations.c: This factor affects Rf values but is not the only one.
explanations.d: Correct. Solvent polarity, chamber saturation, and pH can all influence Rf values.

questions.question: If TLC spots are too high, i.e., too near the solvent front, then what can I do now?

explanations.a: Incorrect. Increasing polarity usually makes spots move even higher.
explanations.b: Correct. Decreasing solvent polarity generally lowers the Rf value and improves separation.
explanations.c: May help in some cases, but it is not the primary solution.
explanations.d: Incorrect because increasing solvent polarity is not appropriate here.

questions.question: Do impurities affect the Rf values?

explanations.a: Incorrect. Impurities do not always affect Rf values.
explanations.b: Correct. Depending on the nature and amount of impurities, Rf values may be altered.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Impurities can affect chromatographic behavior.
